acceptance_targets: [ch17_craft_001] Pass if the revised exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:249 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:277 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:341 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:348 lets a reader point to one explicit fact about Saleha's lived danger or death and one explicit practical consequence the forged father line bought Nura. Fail if the passage still relies only on generalized phrases about men, hunger, truth, or safety.

[ch17_dialogue_001] Pass if the revised Halima turns in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:261 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:277 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:339 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:348 still convey the same admissions but no longer read as stand-alone wisdom lines that the narrator or another elder could say. Fail if the exchange still pivots on generalized aphorisms rather than Halima-specific diction.

[ch17_prose_001] Pass if the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:63, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:197 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:201, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:273, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:347, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:379 no longer rely on the same clipped "Not X. Not Y." or "No X. No Y." pattern in more than two places. Fail if three or more of those cited beats still use the same negation-fragment formula.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:249 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:277 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:341 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:348 lets a reader point to one explicit fact about Saleha's lived danger or death and one explicit practical consequence the forged father line bought Nura. Fail if the passage still relies only on generalized phrases about men, hunger, truth, or safety.
findings.problem: When Nura explicitly asks for the straight version of Saleha, Monir, and the forged father line, the chapter answers with thematic generalities such as "young body," "what men and hunger do to girls," and "I wrote you safer" instead of at least one concrete social or bodily fact. That softens the ugliest part of Halima's choice at the exact point the scene should stop flinching, so the central reckoning reads as sanitization drift rather than full presence.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the confession beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:249, especially the turns at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:257, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:273, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:347. Keep Halima's self-accusing stance, but replace part of the summary language with concrete remembered fact: one specific thing Saleha endured in the marriage or in the lead-up to her death, one specific reason Monir was unusable as a father, and one concrete protection the false line bought Nura in house, school, or clerk terms.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised Halima turns in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:261 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:277 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:339 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:348 still convey the same admissions but no longer read as stand-alone wisdom lines that the narrator or another elder could say. Fail if the exchange still pivots on generalized aphorisms rather than Halima-specific diction.
findings.problem: In the chapter's most important exchange, Halima intermittently slips from her style-bible register into polished thesis speech: "A father who exists is cheap," "truth had already eaten enough women in this family," and "what men and hunger do to girls." The style bible asks for Halima's private speech to move through body, texture, and compromise rather than overt philosophy; here several turns sound like the novel summarizing itself instead of Halima speaking under pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:265, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:273, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:339, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:347, recast at least two of Halima's maxim-like lines into harsher, more local speech rooted in labor, household, or bodily memory. Let her qualify, interrupt herself, or reach for concrete comparison instead of delivering portable aphorisms.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:63, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:197 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:201, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:273, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:347, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:379 no longer rely on the same clipped "Not X. Not Y." or "No X. No Y." pattern in more than two places. Fail if three or more of those cited beats still use the same negation-fragment formula.
findings.problem: The chapter repeatedly leans on the same clipped negation construction for emphasis: "Not repair. Not nothing.", "No acceptance. No refusal. No performance.", "Not absolution. Nothing so grand.", "Not noble. Not clean.", "Not weakly. Not falsely.", and "Not forgiveness. Not even peace." Because this pattern appears across multiple climactic beats, it stops feeling deliberate and becomes a prose tic. It also brushes against the constitution's ban on elliptical clipping in narration.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the emphasis beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:63, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:197, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:201, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:273, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:347, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:379. Keep one or two fragmentary denials if you want the cadence, but rewrite the rest as full sentences or materially different rhythms so each turn earns its own emphasis.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: Lean into contradiction: mercy that creates future damage, tenderness inside humiliation, and paperwork treated as an instrument of violence. In edge cases, choose the more socially and bodily specific version over the tasteful summary or thematic paraphrase.
aesthetic_risk_policy.edge_case_resolution: When a scene risks becoming too neat, keep the hurt visible and let no one explain it away too quickly. When a scene risks becoming lurid, keep it tethered to character perception, material fact, and consequence rather than spectacle.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: If bodily pain, sexual threat, shame, hunger, or bureaucratic exclusion matter in a scene, they must register in specific lived detail; vague softening counts as a failed draft.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High in rendered English dialogue; contractions are the norm, with fuller forms reserved for emphasis, institutional performance, or character-specific formality.
dialogue_rules.dialect_rendering_policy: Render Bangla and char-specific speech through rhythm, hierarchy, metaphor, and social assumption, not through phonetic misspelling or exoticized broken English.
dialogue_rules.subtext_requirement: Most important exchanges should carry a visible practical agenda and a second, less speakable pressure beneath it, especially around kinship, shame, and money.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate public pressure with private reckoning, crowd scenes with object-heavy aftermath, and dialogue-dense chapters with chapters driven by inventory, travel, weather, or delayed information. No two consecutive chapters should rely on the same scene count, confrontation style, or emotional tempo.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plainspoken but exact, letting bureaucratic vocabulary and domestic vocabulary sit in the same paragraph without either swallowing the other; lyric lift should emerge from concrete detail, not ornamental abstraction.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third with one focal consciousness per scene, anchored primarily to Halima and opening selectively to Nura or Kashem only where the outline assigns that pressure.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Flexible sentence music: longer accrual sentences for weather, labor, and memory; short declarative cuts for administrative harm, revelation, and emotional impact.
